project_overview.project_name: Somalia - Horn of Africa Infrastructure Integration Project (SHIIP)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To prepare a spatially coordinated investment pipeline of economic corridor projects, and to strengthen the national government's management of selected sectors
project_overview.total_financing: US$63 million (SDR 43.9 million IDA grant + US$5 million Horn of Africa MDTF)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Direct: FGS ministries (MPWRH, MTCA, MPMT, MCT, MEWR, MFMR, MCI); Indirect: 2.1 million Somalis within 5km of corridors, 8.5 million in connected cities; Downstream: entire Somali population through economic corridor development

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Somalia is classified as conflict-affected state; Al-Shabaab controls significant rural territory and conducts attacks in urban areas; protracted conflict since 1991; security situation particularly challenging in central and southern parts; project includes security management planning

key_components.description: Identification and preparation of regional economic corridor projects including technical design of ~550km of road works, environmental and social management instruments, trade facilitation diagnostics, economic analysis, and design of aligned digital/energy infrastructure. Includes climate-resilient design considerations.
key_components.financing: US$30 million (Regional IDA US$16.8M + National IDA US$8.4M + MDTF US$4.8M)

key_components.description: National-level program for ~900km of secondary/tertiary roads linked to corridors, transmission lines and digital development, maritime port assessments, fisheries sector economic assessment, and area-wide trade facilitation analyses. Climate resilience integrated in designs.

Support for FGS institutional capacity in transport and trade sectors including: National Transport Policy development, Road Sector Strategy, National Ports Masterplan, Highway Authority and Port Authority frameworks, trade facilitation programs, integrated corridor planning, digital/energy/fisheries sector planning, environmental and social risk management capacity building, and gender/social inclusion analysis.
key_components.financing: US$7.8 million (Regional IDA US$1.0M + National IDA US$6.6M + MDTF US$0.2M)

key_components.description: Project Coordination Unit establishment and operation within MPWRH including staff recruitment (coordinator, procurement, financial management, environmental/social safeguards, economist, security officer, communications specialist), technical support consultancy with draw-down mechanism, monitoring and evaluation, and capacity building support.
key_components.financing: US$7.2 million (Regional IDA US$2.2M + National IDA US$5.0M)

key_components.description: Allows reallocation of uncommitted project funds to support emergency response and reconstruction following eligible crisis or emergency, subject to CERC Manual and Emergency Action Plan preparation.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Public Works, Reconstruction and Housing (MPWRH) with involvement of beneficiary ministries: MPMT, MTCA, MCT, MEWR, MFMR, MCI; oversight by MoF and MPIED; financial management through External Assistance Fiduciary Section (EAFS) in MoF
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee co-chaired by Ministers of MPWRH and Finance, including all beneficiary ministry ministers; convenes annually or as needed; Project Coordination Unit acts as Secretariat; beneficiary agencies responsible for technical inputs and approval of deliverables
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF (November 2020); Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) and Procurement Plan prepared; all procurement activities subject to prior World Bank review; use of World Bank Standard Procurement Documents customized to national standards; STEP tool for tracking; emphasis on knowledge transfer to government staff; mitigation of risks through dedicated Procurement Specialist, training, and probity advisors where appropriate
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: PCU responsible for results monitoring; data collection largely built into planned studies; annual reporting on PDO and intermediate results indicators; monitoring of environmental and social commitments through ESCP; stakeholder engagement monitoring per SEP; grievance redress mechanism at FGS level with district/FMS level stakeholder meetings

identified_risks.risk_description: Al-Shabaab controls significant rural territory and conducts attacks; fragile FGS-FMS relationships; political competition over resources; unclear jurisdictional responsibilities among ministries; recent electoral turmoil threatens political stability and FGS institutional sustainability
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Regular stakeholder communication on project activities and allocations; engagement with FGS offices and FMS; security management planning; incorporation of security considerations in project readiness screening and corridor prioritization; security management plans for follow-on projects

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak institutional capacity for implementation given conflict legacy; limited experience with World Bank procedures; nascent government institutions; weak sectoral agencies; limited human resource capital; deficient capacity at federal and state levels
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruitment of qualified consultants and PCU staff; regular training and support through project management and institutional strengthening; intensive support at key implementation stages; hands-on approach with learning-by-doing; external technical assistance with draw-down mechanism; knowledge transfer requirements in ToRs; capacity building integrated into study preparation

identified_risks.risk_description: High potential for fraud and corruption; weak financial management and procurement systems; limited number of competent procurement staff; weak capacity of bidders; high price bids due to security; collusion among bidders; inadequate contract management experience; weak legal and financial institutions; AML/CTF concerns
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Leverage centralized EAFS system with track record of success; 100% prior review of procurement activities; dedicated Procurement and Financial Management specialists in PCU; training on World Bank procedures; third-party monitoring agents; multi-layered controls; direct payment modalities to minimize designated account holdings; frequent monitoring of fund flows; on-job training and mentoring; probity advisors where appropriate; awareness campaigns on integrity; support for procurement reforms

identified_risks.risk_description: High environmental and social risks from downstream projects including: loss of vegetation, soil erosion, pollution, public health impacts, traffic hazards, noise, dust, e-waste; social risks include security threats to project workers, exclusion of vulnerable groups, elite capture, land acquisition and displacement, gender-based violence, labor influx, child labor, human trafficking, occupational health and safety issues; weak government capacity for E&S risk management; limited access for community consultations due to insecurity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Preparation of Environmental and Social Review Summary, Stakeholder Engagement Plan, Environmental and Social Commitment Plan, Labor Management Plan prior to appraisal; development of ESMF, RPF, Framework for SHULTC/Minorities, Security Management Framework prior to RFP issuance; Strategic Environmental and Social Assessment with National Transport Policy; Environmental and Social Risk Capacity Enhancement Action Plan within 6 months of effectiveness; gender assessments and sex-disaggregated data collection; GBV/SEA risk mitigation action plan; security management plans for project and follow-on activities; grievance redress mechanism with confidentiality for GBV/SEAH complaints; stakeholder engagement throughout project lifecycle

identified_risks.risk_description: Protracted conflict and insecurity in central and southern Somalia; Al-Shabaab insurgency; attacks on urban areas; security risks to project staff and consultants; limited access to project sites; danger of kidnapping and armed group attacks; security premium on costs
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Two-tier security approach: (1) SHIIP security arrangements including Security Management Plan, World Bank security officer in Mogadishu, security arrangements for supervision missions, security considerations in readiness screening; (2) Planning for secure implementation of follow-on activities including Security Management Plans for future projects, security lens in prioritization methodology considering both implementation risks and potential for corridor to improve regional security, incorporation of security considerations in strategies and policies

identified_risks.risk_description: Extremely high demand for development with limited resources; competing centers of power (FGS, FMS, clan affiliations, armed groups); risk of project objections and delays; potential for selection bias and elite capture; exclusion of poor and vulnerable groups; complex clan-based dynamics
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Sustained formal and informal consultations; prioritization methodology including consultation process engaging FMS and local levels; local consultations during design preparation; Stakeholder Engagement Plan with monitoring indicators; formal engagement events with participation tracking by stakeholder category; grievance redress mechanism; regular stakeholder meetings at district and FMS levels

identified_risks.risk_description: COVID-19 pandemic impacts; rising inflation and food shortages from Ukraine war and drought; limited fiscal space; low domestic revenue collection (3.1% of GDP); constrained government resources; recurrent expenditures crowding out infrastructure investment
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design focuses on preparation of investment-ready projects to attract external financing; economic analysis of corridors to ensure viability; prioritization methodology including sustainability considerations; performance-based contracting approach for future works; coordination with other development partners to leverage resources

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak sectoral policies and legal frameworks; unclear demarcation of authorities between FGS and FMS; absence of clear regulations; nascent institutions not yet operationalized; limited analytical underpinnings for sector management
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Development of National Transport Policy, Road Sector Strategy, Ports and Maritime Transport Strategy, National Highway Authority and Port Authority frameworks; preparation of legal, regulatory and institutional frameworks; development of integrated national infrastructure investment strategy; harmonization of sectoral data into interactive platform; support for policy coordination among HoA countries

identified_risks.risk_description: Limited technical capacity for infrastructure planning and design; weak standards and specifications; absence of road asset management strategies; limited experience with climate-resilient design; coordination challenges across sectors
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Road designs conforming to good international practice with climate-informed considerations; coordination of roadworks with fiber optic and power line designs at early stage; application of 'dig once' principle; integration of climate resilience in design standards; road safety considerations including audits and blackspot identification; gender and social inclusion analysis in designs; performance-based contracting approach incorporating maintenance

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: High (reflecting potential cumulative environmental risks from downstream projects despite no civil works in SHIIP itself)
triggered_safeguard_policies: Projects on International Waterways (OP 7.50) - Yes, exception approved for Gulf of Aden
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Centralized grievance redress mechanism at FGS level; district and FMS level regular stakeholder meetings; confidentiality provisions for GBV/SEAH complaints; World Bank Grievance Redress Service available; Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service) available
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Only 37% of women participate in labor market vs 58% of men; men participate 34 times more than women in transport/communications sectors; women under-represented in maritime and fisheries sectors; gender-based violence including sexual exploitation and abuse is significant challenge; 73-78% of GBV survivors are female IDPs; project will conduct gender assessments, collect sex-disaggregated data, analyze gender gaps in employment and barriers to benefit from infrastructure projects, design gender-responsive studies, include gender considerations in feasibility studies and road safety studies, target 30% female staff in PCU
vulnerable_groups: Poor and extremely poor (69% poverty rate nationally)
Displaced women and girls (most vulnerable to GBV)
Children (risk of child labor in labor influx scenarios)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for fragile and conflict-affected context; incorporates conflict sensitivity through: (1) security management planning for project implementation; (2) security considerations in project readiness screening and corridor prioritization; (3) potential for infrastructure development to improve security and stability in regions through increased economic interdependence; (4) incorporation of security considerations in national strategies and policies; (5) recognition that infrastructure development can serve as visible representation of state influence and control; (6) careful site selection considering both security risks to implementation and potential for corridor to improve regional security; (7) engagement with security forces and liaison arrangements
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Somalia emerging from 30 years of violent conflict and fragility since 1991; fragmented by armed conflict, regime changes, and state capture; recent political settlement with 2012 federal government transfer and 2017 peaceful election; federation process complex with 5 Federal Member States plus Somaliland; ongoing political instability and disagreements over power/resource sharing; Al-Shabaab insurgency controls significant rural territory; weak government institutions still nascent; limited fiscal capacity; high poverty (69%), food insecurity (2.5 million), displacement (3.0 million IDPs, 777,000 refugees); recurring droughts and floods; low infrastructure development; limited institutional capacity for service delivery; governance challenges including corruption and weak rule of law
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Protracted conflict and insecurity particularly in central and southern Somalia; Al-Shabaab retains control of significant rural territory and frequently launches attacks on urban areas; security risks to project staff and consultants including danger of attacks and kidnapping; limited access to project sites for stakeholder engagement; security premium on project costs; project incorporates: Security Management Plan for SHIIP, World Bank security officer in Mogadishu, security arrangements for supervision missions, security considerations in readiness screening, security management plans for follow-on projects, security lens in prioritization methodology, incorporation of security considerations in national strategies
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Somalia has 3.0 million internally displaced persons and 777,000 refugees outside country; displacement driven by conflict, droughts, and floods; majority of drought-related displacement in rural areas with movement to urban centers (Mogadishu, Baidoa, Kismayo host 40% of drought-related displacements); livelihoods of refugees and migrants dependent on cross-border trade; project addresses displacement through: improved connectivity and infrastructure to support livelihoods, border region development to create sense of belonging and inclusion, resilience building, improved access to services, potential for infrastructure to reduce vulnerability to climate shocks; land acquisition and involuntary resettlement identified as potential risk for downstream projects with Resettlement Planning Framework to be prepared
